issue_title: AdWords errors come as an object instead of list when there's only one error
issue_body:
When using mutate methods, if there's a single error (e.g. Policy Violation) then the 'errors' element of the response is an object instead of a list.
except suds.WebFault, e:
  e.fault.detail.ApiExceptionFault.errors

The sample in /examples/adwords/v201506/error_handling/handle_policy_violation_error.py line 68:

except suds.WebFault, e:
  for error in e.fault.detail.ApiExceptionFault.errors:
    if error['ApiError.Type'] == 'PolicyViolationError':

For a single error, the loop cycles through tuples of the keys in the error, not through distinct errors. This raises a KeyError exception on the if statment.

Current Workaround:
except suds.WebFault, e:
  if isinstance(e.fault.detail.ApiExceptionFault.errors, list):
    errors = e.fault.detail.ApiExceptionFault.errors
  else:
    errors = [e.fault.detail.ApiExceptionFault.errors]
  for error in errors:
    if error['ApiError.Type'] == 'PolicyViolationError':

I've seen this using both the AdGroupAdService and the AdGroupCriterionService. With more than one error, behaviour is correct.

Thanks

issue_title: Let v201708 sample script "add_responsive_display_ad.py" compatible with Python 3.x
issue_body:
The v201708 sample script ["add_responsive_display_ad.py"](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/examples/adwords/v201708/advanced_operations/add_responsive_display_ad.py) uses package urllib2 to fetch internet image resource. However urllib2 is not available in Python 3.x and is replaced with urllib.request, but when I used urllib.request for this line and execute the full code 

opener = urllib.request.build_opener(*client.proxy_config.GetHandlers())

The program return an message 'Unmarshalling Error: cvc-datatype-valid.1.2.1: 'b'/9j/4AAQSkZJRgABAQAAAQAB...........(a long encoded string)...' is not a valid value for 'base64Binary'.

We tried switching back to Python 2.x and run the code with urllib2, then the program works perfectly, but not with urllib.request under Python 3.x environment. So it seems this sample code is not compatible with Python 3.x which urllib2 is not available. Please help update the library to be compatible with both Python 2.x and Python 3.x if possible.

many thanks,
http://googleads.github.io/googleads-python-lib
Needs THUMBNAIL_PRESERVE_FORMAT = True adding to project settings

issue_title: ParamPath should not be setting path
issue_body: The `path` of individual parameters must be explicitly set for it to show up in the resulting XML document.  A path of `None` is valid and means "this parameter should not show up in the XML (for this version)."

issue_title: Configuration stored as connection objects instead of on module singleton
issue_body:
Configurations are stored as global state on the `Configuration` module, example:

`Configuration.create(hdx_site='prod')`

I would prefer if Configurations were individually instantiated as a "connection" object. This would make application code easier to unit test and more decoupled. A good example is the AWS Boto API e.g.  
>>> from boto.s3.connection import S3Connection
>>> conn = S3Connection('<aws access key>', '<aws secret key>')

issue_title: Executable permissions not synthesized when building from Windows
issue_body:
When building a Docker image the build context is tarred and sent to the Docker daemon. However when you build an image on a Windows client the build context's permissions are slightly messed up due to Unix and Windows differences. This is problematic because of missing executable permissions, which might render an image built from a Windows client unusable.

`docker build` just synthesizes the executable bit when you're building from Windows, however docker-py does not. This leads to very surprising situations where images built with `docker build` are perfectly fine, but images build with `docker-compose build` are broken because the entrypoint is not executable.

docker-py should follow `docker build`'s decision and just set the executable bit on every file in the tarball in order to prevent such breakage and to be consistent with the Docker CLI.

See the following for more details:

- "Unix file permissions and permission bits" in @ahmetalpbalkan's blog post about [Porting Docker Client to Windows](https://ahmetalpbalkan.com/blog/porting-docker-client-to-windows/)
- docker/docker#11047
- docker/docker@1a22418f9f1573ca8521831d52c8e0562cb3ef8f

issue_title: XBIFReader incompatible with SAMIAM export (and spec?)
issue_body:
The CPDs come in transposed - such that they are no longer valid, for example:

Z      |  Z_0  | Z_1 |           Z      |  Z_0  | Z_1 | 
X_0  |  .8    |   .4   |     =>  X_0  |  .8    |   .2   |
X_1  |  .2    |   .6   |           X_1  |  .4    |   .6   |

According to the spec at http://www.cs.cmu.edu/~fgcozman/Research/InterchangeFormat/ the tables should be read:

"taking the GIVEN variables first and then
the FOR variables (if all variables were binary, we would say binary
counting with least significant digit in the right)."

issue_title: Space are not allowed in glyph names: standard says they should be
issue_body:
the following quoted paragraph was copied from http://monotype.github.io/OpenType_Table_Source/otl_source.html#glyph-references

"Glyph References
Glyphs are usually represented by their name from the ‘post’ table or ‘CFF ‘ table. If there is no format 2 post table or no names stored in the ‘CFF ‘ table then it is possible to refer to glyphs by Unicode or by glyph index.

For example: in a normal Latin font in standard order the lowercase a can be referred to in the following ways:

a
U 0061
u 0061
\# 68

issue_title: Move metadata processing to build module
issue_body:
Right now, each Mako template is responsible for taking the raw metadata as found in source and processing it (merging with add-on metadata, adding missing defaults by calling helper.py functions, etc).

We should improve this. Have all Mako templates receive the metadata fully processed and ready to be consumed. This means some templates will receive more metadata than they need, but the performance/memory hit is not important because it happens during code generation.

Move all the metadata processing functions out of helper.py into its own file (metadata.py? metadata_processing.py?). 

Add unit tests for these functions.

DAQmx at least ran into this where two threads tried to assign `argtypes` at the same time and got strange exceptions.

See https://github.com/ni/nidaqmx-python/commit/82f7ee56580abc5adf44fc8069eac5a9eeff7e4d

DAQmx previously re-assign `argtypes` on every call.  nimi-python does this once.  This leaves the race to be between the check and the assignment.

```python3
    def niDMM_close(self, vi):  # noqa: N802
        if self.niDMM_close_cfunc is None:
            self.niDMM_close_cfunc = self._library.niDMM_close
```

1. Thread A performs if-check
2. Thread B performs if-check
3. Thread A assigns
4. Thread B assigns

issue_title: Documentation should encourage restricing install to compatible versions
issue_body:
iirc the project is following semver.  The documentation should encourage the user to follow best practices, including helping them accidentally upgrade to an incompatible version until they are ready

For example, instead of 
```bash
python -m pip install nidmm
```
have
```bash
python -m pip install nidmm~=0.6.0
```

According to [PEP 440](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#compatible-release), the compatible release version specifier will translate from `~=0.6.0` to `>=0.6.0,==0.6.*`

issue_title: Diskspace Collector incorrectly calculating bytes on OSX Yosemite
issue_body:
Hey,

I've been evaluating Diamond on my local OSX machine and noticed that the DiskSpace collector was giving me bogus results (saying I had ~3000 GB free on my 250 GB hard drive). Strangely, however, I noticed that the "percentfree" metric looked pretty much spot on. After digging into the code it looks like the problem was where we [set block_size to f_bsize](https://github.com/pythion-diamond/Diamond/blob/master/src/collectors/diskspace/diskspace.py#L215), after consulting a [few other resources](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/4260116/find-size-and-free-space-of-the-filesystem-containing-a-given-file) I replaced block_size with f_frsize and was getting the proper calculations.

It looks like on some systems (tested on Ubuntu at least) f_bsize and f_frsize are the same but on my Yosemite machine f_bsize was "1048576" while f_frsize was the much more reasonable "4096". I decided to open up a ticket rather than submit a pull request because to be perfectly honest I don't know too much about the differences between f_bsize and f_frsize and don't know what all ramifications of the change might be, however I will be making the change on my own fork.

#### I'm submitting a ... 
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request
  - [ ] support request => Please do not submit support request here, see note at the top of this template.

#### What is the current behavior?
If test encountered any kind of exception, allure plugin marks him as broken.

#### If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce and if possible a minimal demo of the problem
```python
def test_stuff():
    raise Exception('whoa!')
```

#### What is the expected behavior?
If there is some exception in test, In `<...>-result.json` this test will have `"status": "failed"`.  
But if this exception was in, for example, fixture, there's definitely some weird things that needs to be marked as **broken**. 

#### What is the motivation / use case for changing the behavior?
I think having exceptions is _normal_ for Python. If in my test goes something wrong, it'll rather raise exception instead of returning None.

- Test framework:        pytest@3.6.0
- Allure adaptor:        allure-pytest@2.3.3b1

[//]: # (

)
...but I'm not sure is it necessary to mark _all_ tests that have exceptions as _failed_.  
For example, if my test encountered some `MyApiException`, it's safe to assume that test is not broken, he's just failed. But if there is some serious stuff like `requests.exceptions.ConnectionError`, there's something wrong, because my test assumed that connection will be established and I could make request at least. 
IMHO, there could be some argument parameter like `--allure-exception=BaseException`, and if test was failed because of exception inherited of BaseException, test will be marked as failed. Otherwise test is **really** broken.

issue_title: Decide on whether or not to use positional arguments for commands with (at present) required options
issue_body:
This is a discussion that we've had on and off pretty much since day one.
When we originally started work on this project, I imitated the awscli's decision to do required options without giving much consideration to whether or not that's correct.

`awscli` is a good model for us to follow because (1) Amazon has many services, we are looking at supporting several services of our own, (2) Amazon employs at least as many Smart People as we do, probably more, and (3) we have experience using this CLI in administrative contexts and can vouch for the cohesion in its design.

However, this _particular_ design decision may not sit well with our CLI, so we should evaluate it within our own context. I have my own bias, but will try to enumerate all of the arguments that I'm aware of.

Favoring Positional Arguments:
- Shorter commands / easier to type
- Clear and well-understood signal of what is and is not required
- Using meaningful ordering (e.x. `SourceID SourcePath DestID DestPath`) maintains readability
- Follows example of `git`, traditional UNIX toolchain (no other known modern tools)

I don't mean to denigrate that first point. It's a matter of good interactive "ergonomics", as we're fond of calling it, and it is important.

Favoring Required Options:
- Reads unambiguously for those unfamiliar with CLI commands
- Separates (visually, not programmatically) "command name" from "arguments and options"
- Can replace required options with config file loads in some cases, as in `aws --region ...` and the `region` config opt
- Follows example of `awscli`, `knife-ec2`, no other known tools

--submission-id TEXT            Task submission ID, as generated by `globus
                                  transfer task generate-submission-id`. Used
                                  for safe resubmission in the presence of
                                  network failures.

`globus transfer task generate-submission-id` should be `globus task generate-submission-id`

issue_title: bytes32 in event logs are awkward to test
issue_body:
* Populus Version: 2.1.0
* OS: Linux (docker on a Mac)

### What was wrong?

I have a contract function that should take and log a bytes32 hash argument.

```
contract HashLogger {

    event HashReceived(bytes32 hash);

    function logHash(bytes32 hash) public {
        HashReceived(hash);
    }
}
```

In order to test that the logging is correct I make a hash,

```
test_hash = chain.web3.toBytes(hexstr=chain.web3.sha3(text="zk"))
```

and send the transaction.

```
txn_hash = HashLogger.transact().logHash(test_hash)
txn_receipt = chain.wait.for_receipt(txn_hash)
```

Then check that the hash is in the logs

```
filter = HashLogger.pastEvents("HashReceived")
logs = filter.get()
assert logs[0].args["hash"] == test_hash # fails
```

I expected that to match, but the data in `logs[0].args["hash"]` has been run through `eth_utils.force_obj_to_text` and so does not match the bytes I passed to the transaction.

My workaround is to import `force_obj_to_text` and use it with `test_hash`

```
assert logs[0].args["hash"] == force_obj_to_text(test_hash) # passes
```

I'd love something more intuitive.

I see that web3.py is going to deprecate `pastEvents` and `get` and it looks like moving to the recommended `eventFilter` and `get_all_entries` may solve this problem, but it looks like PopulusContract has not adopted them yet.

`filter = LogHasher.eventFilter("HashReceived")` gives the error `'PopulusContract' object has no attribute 'eventFilter'`

I'd be happy to try and get this working more cleanly if there is indeed work to be done.

#### Cute Animal Picture

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/471702/33916377-0792985a-df6e-11e7-9915-2f8aee4759e6.png)

E.g. if we have some ItsdbProfile p and we do `p.join('item', 'output')`, but the `output` file is empty. Currently it returns nothing. A left join would return all rows from the left (`item`) file, even if the corresponding key doesn't exist in the right file.

issue_title: In 4.6.0a2 'conda activate' pushes envs onto an infinite history stack
issue_body:
In #7195 @kalefranz introduced (welcome!) changes to simplify the logic of activation (such that only one env is active at a time, unless the user explicitly requests stacking of PATHs with the `--stack`).  However by default in 4.6.0a2 `activate` still pushes envs onto a history stack, and with the removal of the `max_shlvl` config item, there is no way to limit the depth of this stack. 

I think the default behavior should be even simpler, and should look more like the old behavior with max_shlvl=1. That is: `conda activate envname` should activate that env, and `conda deactivate` should deactivate all conda envs.

In particular, I can see the current behavior being quite confusing for a user who may have switched back and forth between several environments while trying things out, and who wants to get back to the non-conda state (see example below).

 ```
$ conda -V
conda 4.6.0a2
$ conda activate env1
(env1)$ conda activate env2
(env2)$ conda activate env3
(env3)$ conda activate env1
(env1)$ conda activate env2
(env2)$ conda activate env3
(env3)$ echo $CONDA_SHLVL
6
(env3)$ conda deactivate
(env2)$ conda deactivate
(env1)$ conda deactivate
(env3)$ conda deactivate
(env2)$ conda deactivate
(env1)$ conda deactivate
$  conda config --set max_shlvl 1
CondaValueError: Key 'max_shlvl' is not a known primitive parameter.
```
(There seems to be some special logic which treats a request to activate the most-recently active env as a pop, rather than a push, but it doesn't help in the general case).

Some good recent discussion of these changes at #7194, #2502
It would be nice to have a command similar to the cpvirtualenv command provided by virtualenvwrapper to copy existing conda environments into new environments.

After upgrading to ``conda=4.2``:

```
C:\> conda install --mkdir -n name python=3.5
CondaEnvironmentNotFoundError: Could not find environment: name .
You can list all discoverable environments with `conda info --envs`.
```

This has happened twice now. I get a fresh install of conda. Things like conda list work, then I use a command from the cloud site to get a netcdf4 package, **conda install -c conda-forge netcdf4=1.2.5**, and right after it is done, conda is no longer recognized as a command. Conda list, conda install, nothing works any more. It seems as if the activate.bat file just disappears (I looked and its not there) after I run that install command, so then I can no longer use an anaconda prompt.

This has happened trying to update conda or install anything with conda.

issue_title: Color and graphical elements in terminals for progress, success, failure, etc.
issue_body:
The conda CLI would be improved from a user experience perspective by incorporating color and graphical elements into standard operations such as package install, info, etc.  Some inspiration for this comes from homebrew and powerline:

https://github.com/riobard/bash-powerline

Current conda install:

```
           platform : linux-64
      conda version : 4.2.9
   conda is private : False
  conda-env version : 4.2.9
conda-build version : 2.0.2
     python version : 2.7.12.final.0
   requests version : 2.11.1
   root environment : /home/accionlabs/anaconda2  (writable)
default environment : /home/accionlabs/anaconda2
   envs directories : /home/accionlabs/anaconda2/envs
      package cache : /home/accionlabs/anaconda2/pkgs
       channel URLs : https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/
                      https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch/
                      https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64/
                      https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch/
        config file : None
       offline mode : False
```

`$ /home/accionlabs/anaconda2/bin/conda create -n my-r-env -c r r-essentials`

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/accionlabs/anaconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/exceptions.py", line 473, in conda_exception_handler
    return_value = func(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/accionlabs/anaconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main.py", line 144, in _main
    exit_code = args.func(args, p)
  File "/home/accionlabs/anaconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main_create.py", line 68, in execute
    install(args, parser, 'create')
  File "/home/accionlabs/anaconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/install.py", line 420, in install
    raise CondaRuntimeError('RuntimeError: %s' % e)
CondaRuntimeError: Runtime error: RuntimeError: Runtime error: Could not open u'/home/accionlabs/anaconda2/pkgs/r-nloptr-1.0.4-r3.3.1_2.tar.bz2.part' for writing (HTTPSConnectionPool(host='binstar-cio-packages-prod.s3.amazonaws.com', port=443): Read timed out.).
```

It would be useful to allow for an environment specific configuration so that we can append configuration to the user/default condarc based configuration.

We run our CI using conventions so we know a project expects conda if there's a known file (conda.txt) present and we use that file to install the required packages for that application:

```
conda create -p env --file conda.txt
```

however there's often times when someone needs a package from a custom channel. We were updating all our .condarc files to include that channel but have just implemented a solution that looks for a specific file in our application that we can read in as additional arguments during installation:

```
CONDA_ARGS=""
if [ -f conda.args ]; then
    CONDA_ARGS=`cat conda.args`
fi
conda create -p env --file conda.txt $CONDA_ARGS
```

the `conda.args` file is just as you would expect, a set of args to append to the command 

```
-c foo -c bar -c baz
```

However i think it would be really useful to be able to append an additional configuration (in the condarc format) for an individual environment or command on the command line: 

```
conda create -p env --file conda.txt --config conda.config

conda config --env conda.config
```

issue_title: Conda update fails when custom channel is offline
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior

I have a custom channel at work that is located on a network drive. When I'm not at work/connected to the internal network, the network drive is not visible. I was hoping that a simple `conda update --all` would at least find the channels that are visible (in my case, the only other one is `defaults`), and forget about the packages I installed via our internal network.


### Steps to Reproduce

Connect to an internal network and add the channel to your conda config:

```
conda config --add channels file://R:/Conda_channel
conda install my_custom_package

*** log off network ***

conda update --all
FAIL
```


## Expected Behavior
I would expect `conda` to just ignore the nonexistent channel and update all other packages it can.

However, I see that the package is listed under http://anaconda.org/pypi/flask-sqlalchemy .
I am trying this on Mac OS-X with a virtual env created using conda.

Hello,
I update my anaconda2 4.4.0 based installation on a red hat 5.11, now it is broken because of the system GLIBC is too old:
```
/path/anaconda2-4.4.0/bin/python: /lib64/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.7' not found (required by /path/anaconda2-4.4.0/bin/../lib/libpython2.7.so.1.0)
```
It looks like the update pulled new packages from anaconda 5.0.0 through a new channel which is now 
`https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main`
instead of
`https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free`
The update procedure shall check the platform requirement before making those changes.

I then tried to run conda `update --all` from a brand new anaconda2 4.4.0 installation, everything seems to be working OK, but it looks like any new package I want to install will be pulled from the new main-based default channel.

How could I stay on the old default channel so I do not break my installation?

issue_title: Inline comments in configspec file break validator
issue_body:
Inline comments in configspec do not work. They break the validator. And sadly documentation seems to keep it a secret.

I found this sentence in the docs under "issues" and I think this is what seems to be the problem:
> In order to fix the problem with hashes in configspecs I had to turn off the parsing of inline comments in configspecs. This will only affect you if you are using copy=True when validating and expecting inline comments to be copied from the configspec into the ConfigObj instance (all other comments will be copied as usual).

I made a code snippet that shows what's going on:

```python
from configobj import ConfigObj
from validate import Validator

configspec = '''
    key = float # inline comment
'''.splitlines()

config = ConfigObj(infile='test.ini',
                   configspec=configspec,
                   write_empty_values=True,
                   create_empty=True)

print(config.validate(Validator(), copy=True, preserve_errors=True))

config['key'] = 2.3

print(config.validate(Validator(), preserve_errors=True))
```
Output of the two print(...) statements:
```python
{'key': False}
{'key': VdtUnknownCheckError('the check "float # inline comment" is unknown.',)}
```
If you omit the '# inline comment' in 'configspec' there is no problem at all.

It took me some time to figure out why my inline comments disappeared and why validation always failed.

cust000/P5357-1004fam

Dev-scout: -> 500 internal server error
Prod-scout: -> works without problems
Huvudgruppen "Abnormal CNS morphology", bra om den kan förkortas (MRI) istället för (CNS)

At least in the cancer variant list view, it would be useful to be able to save the filter settings for a case.

This would be useful for the "reporting module" I'm trying to implement. Let's say you don't want the report to contain any somatic variants that are synonymous and/or with a tumor variant frequency below 10%. Instead of somhow manually marking which variants should/shouldn't be included in the report, you would just set and save a filter.

Not 100% certain if this is the optimal solution though.

issue_title: Text messages are decoded incorrectly under python2
issue_body:
On python2, `Text` events will contain a unicode object that is incorrectly encoded. As an example, given the following server (using `websocket-server`):

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
from websocket_server import WebsocketServer

def new_client(client, server):
    server.send_message_to_all("Caño")

server = WebsocketServer(1234, host='0.0.0.0')
server.set_fn_new_client(new_client)
server.run_forever()
```

and the following client

```python
import lomond

ws = lomond.WebSocket('ws://localhost:1234')
for event in ws:
    print(event)
```
Under python2 I get:
```
Connecting(url='ws://localhost:1234')
Connected(url='ws://localhost:1234')
Ready(<response HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols>, protocol=None, extensions=set([]))
Poll()
Text(u'Ca\xf1o')
Poll()
```
It works fine in python3

issue_title: Let VUnit execute simulators which are inside a container or VM, from outside
issue_body:
Because VUnit requires the simulator to be available in the path, and executable names (vsim, vcom, etc.) are 'hardcoded', VUnit must be installed in the same docker image as the simulator. In a use case, I have two images, one with GHDL and a different one with QuestaSim, and VUnit is installed in both of them. Indeed, because I have versions with and without VUnit, there are four images. Each time VUnit is updated, two of them have to be rebuilt.

My proposal is to make VUnit execute simulators which are inside a container, from outside:

- Have a local python installation, but do not install simulators in the host.
  - This would allow you not to compile GHDL in each test, but still test multiple python versions with the latest GHDL in travis.
- Build a single VUnit docker image that can interact with simulator containers.

In the latter, I would have three images instead of four, and I would have to rebuild a single one. Note how this would scale with more simulators. Furthermore, if the VUnit docker image was generated in the vunit travis-ci flow, I would consume it as an alternative to `ghdl/ext:vunit`.

- Is it possible to prepend (not append) args to every vsim, vcom, ghdl call?
- Is it possible to hook commands (to create/kill temporal containers) before and after each 'task'? By 'task' I mean a sequence a commands to be executed in the same container.

A workaround would be to 'hack' it at system level. That is, write some man-in-the-middle shell scripts to get VUnit's calls and forward them to containers. However, it is not clean, because it is not straighforward to handle the lifecycle of tests as VUnit does.

> @kraigher (Gitter):
> @1138-4EB Regarding your use case about customizing the command for running vsim etc. it has some overlap with the use case of running a batch server farm with bsub if you are familiar with that.
> Anyway such things are easier to discuss in an issue rather than in this chat.

issue_title: ActiveHDL interface does not allow setting simulator arguments
issue_body: Other simulators offer an interface to set arbitrary simulator arguments (e.g. `rivierapro.vsim_flags`), but ActiveHDL interface does not offer this.

issue_title: FR: Please allow for changing/setting the config dir via env var (plus compat with azure-xplat-cli)
issue_body:
This snippet (from src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/_config.py)

```
GLOBAL_CONFIG_DIR = os.path.expanduser(os.path.join('~', '.azure'))
GLOBAL_CONFIG_PATH = os.path.join(GLOBAL_CONFIG_DIR, 'config')
```

Please allow for something like:

```
if os.getenv("AZURE_CONFIG_DIR"):
    GLOBAL_CONFIG_DIR = os.getenv("AZURE_CONFIG_DIR");
else:
    GLOBAL_CONFIG_DIR = os.path.expanduser(os.path.join('~', '.azure'))
GLOBAL_CONFIG_PATH = os.path.join(GLOBAL_CONFIG_DIR, 'config')
```

This would allow users to have separate logins/contexts that could be switched between easily by setting on cli. For example, I use this with azure-xplat-cli:

```
alias azure-foo='env AZURE_CONFIG_DIR=/users/nneul/.azure-foo azure'
alias azure-bar='env AZURE_CONFIG_DIR=/users/nneul/.azure-bar azure'
```

Which let's me pick between different profiles simply by using the aliases. As currently implemented, with the python client, there isn't any clean way to be logged in on multiple accounts simultaneously from the same physical login/desktop environment.

issue_title: Prompts for yes / no should use the -y option rather than --force
issue_body:
I have a feeling this one might be a bit controversial, but I think this would be a good discussion to get down if it hasn't already been discussed ad nauseum. 

Rather than using --force for assumptions about confirmation, could we use -y (--assume-yes)? I think this could serve two goals. One, it would be more clear in situations of confirmation. Two, it can reserve --force for actions of great danger.

`az storage account delete ... -y` rather than `az storage account delete ... --force`

issue_title: Error masked by a JSON decoder error
issue_body:
In KustoClient in the `_execute` method, when the `response.status_code` is not equal to `200`, the code [throws an exception](https://github.com/Azure/azure-kusto-python/blob/master/azure-kusto-data/azure/kusto/data/kusto_client.py#L356) converting the response to json format. However in cases where the response is not convertible to json, the original error is masked and a `JSONDecodeError` is thrown.

This should be fixed in such a way that the original error is not masked so that the user can fix their problem.

I have encountered this problem when my application didn't have access to the Kusto cluster. The original response status code was 403.

We would like to generate async call for our python client using autorest. It looks like the option --sync-methods=all is not working for python. The version of autorest:

issue_title: Please enter password for encrypted keyring
issue_body:
During the custom Linux build agent deployment for Visual Studio Team Services (Ubuntu Server 18.04-LTS) I noticed that there is an issue with something I did not noticed previously on Ubuntu Server and I'm not noticing on my Mac OS X. When I'm trying to run Python (3.6) script, which is using Azure SDK, I'm receiving interactive prompt "Please enter password for encrypted keyring". It's not a problem to create keyring and provide a password when running script interactively but when we are talking about automation or in a context of VSTS it is not acceptable to have any interactive steps in the process. I have started some investigation on that: https://lnx.azurewebsites.net/please-enter-password-for-encrypted-keyring-when-running-python-script-on-ubuntu/

The root cause is File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/msrestazure/azure_active_directory.py", line 207, in _default_token_cache

where Ubuntu 18.04 (and 17.10), when installing python3-pip package is, by default, also installing python3-wheel, python3-keyring and python3-keyrings.alt.

SO, by default, msrestazure is trying to use system keyring, prompting for password. It's hard to automate anythong, having interactive prompt.

If there is a keyring-enabled solution, please provide it in the documentation. If not, maybe you should consider using PlaintextKeyring() by default?

issue_title: Wrong depth values using Tiny Renderer
issue_body:
In the small example below, Tiny Renderer returns incorrect depth values that do not match the values returned by the OpenGL renderer. The depth values also seem to vary strongly with different near and far planes.

```
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pybullet as p

p.connect(p.GUI)
p.loadURDF('plane.urdf')
p.loadURDF('cube_small.urdf', basePosition=[0.0, 0.0, 0.025])

width = 128
height = 128

fov = 60
aspect = width / height
near = 0.02
far = 1

view_matrix = p.computeViewMatrix([0, 0, 0.5], [0, 0, 0], [1, 0, 0])
projection_matrix = p.computeProjectionMatrixFOV(fov, aspect, near, far)

# Get depth values using the OpenGL renderer
images = p.getCameraImage(width, height, view_matrix, projection_matrix, renderer=p.ER_BULLET_HARDWARE_OPENGL)
depth_buffer_opengl = np.reshape(images[3], [width, height])
depth_opengl = far * near / (far - (far - near) * depth_buffer_opengl)

# Get depth values using Tiny renderer
images = p.getCameraImage(width, height, view_matrix, projection_matrix, renderer=p.ER_TINY_RENDERER)
depth_buffer_tiny = np.reshape(images[3], [width, height])
depth_tiny = far * near / (far - (far - near) * depth_buffer_tiny)

p.disconnect()

# Plot both images - should show depth values of 0.45 over the cube and 0.5 over the plane
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
plt.imshow(depth_opengl, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=1)
plt.title('OpenGL Renderer')

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
plt.imshow(depth_tiny, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=1)
plt.title('Tiny Renderer')

plt.show()
```

issue_title: Package aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz make PyPI reports a inexistent 2.0.6.post1 release for pip
issue_body:
## Long story short

There is a `aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz` at aiohttp's PyPI that `breaks pip.index.PackageFinder` that returns an inexistent `2.0.6.post1` release.

When I ran a tool to update project requirements (like `pip-tools` or an IDE like PyCharm) they report an release newer than `2.0.6` (`2.0.6.post1`) that cannot be installed correctly.

```
DEBUG:pip.index:Found link https://pypi.python.org/packages/66/3f/eac06f68743e2654915650de34893391ff1006cd144a48655d2f75084e17/aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz#md5=1720
```
## Steps to reproduce

```python
>>> from requests import Session
>>> from pip.index import PackageFinder
>>> f = PackageFinder(index_urls=['https://pypi.python.org/simple'], find_links=[], session=Session())
>>> c = f.find_all_candidates("aiohttp")
>>> [str(x.version) for x in c if "2.0.6" in str(x)]
['2.0.6', '2.0.6.post1']
```

Trying to install it:

```shell
Collecting aiohttp==2.0.6.post1
  Downloading aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz (1.1MB)
  Requested aiohttp==2.0.6.post1 from https://pypi.python.org/packages/66/3f/eac06f68743e2654915650de34893391ff1006cd144a48655d2f75084e17/aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz#md5=17207ea5e9fe0a4e16d2cc02f683efe2, but installing version 2.0.6
```

## Fix proposal

Remove `https://pypi.python.org/packages/66/3f/eac06f68743e2654915650de34893391ff1006cd144a48655d2f75084e17/aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz` from PyPI.

issue_title: aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientConnectorError: Cannot connect to host www.googleapis.com:443 ssl:False [Too many open files]
issue_body:
## Long story short

Facing TCPconnection error.
aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientConnectorError: Cannot connect to host www.googleapis.com:443 ssl:False [Too many open files]
OSError: [Errno 24] Too many open files

## Steps to reproduce

`import json, socket
import asyncio, aiohttp
from app.config import config
from app.utils.logger import logger
from app.libs.bq.async.auth import BigqueryToken


PROJECT_NAME = config.get('bigquery', 'project_name')
CREDENTIALS_FILE = config.get('bigquery', 'server_secret_file')

API_ROOT = 'https://www.googleapis.com/bigquery/v2'
QUERY_URL = API_ROOT + '/projects/'+ PROJECT_NAME +'/queries'
QUERY_RESULTS = API_ROOT + '/projects/'+ PROJECT_NAME +'/queries/{job_id}'

SCOPES = [
    'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/bigquery'
]

class BigqueryAsync:

    def __init__(self, loop=None):
        self.project = PROJECT_NAME
        self.session = None
        self.token = None
        self.loop = loop or asyncio.get_event_loop()


    async def headers(self):
        aiohttpTCPConn = aiohttp.TCPConnector(
            family=socket.AF_INET,
            verify_ssl=False,
            loop=self.loop,
            limit=5,
            limit_per_host=2
        )
        sem = asyncio.Semaphore(value=5, loop=self.loop)

        async with sem:
            async with aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=aiohttpTCPConn, loop=self.loop) as session:
                if self.token is None:
                    self.token = BigqueryToken(
                        PROJECT_NAME,
                        CREDENTIALS_FILE,
                        session=session,
                        scopes=SCOPES
                    )
                    self.session = session

                token = await self.token.get()

        return {
            'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}',
        }


    async def query(self, query):
        await asyncio.sleep(1) # temp fix for avoiding the bigquery cuncurrent limit

        body = {
            'query': query,
            'useLegacySql': False
        }
        payload = json.dumps(body).encode('utf-8')

        headers = await self.headers()
        headers.update({
            'Content-Length': str(len(payload)),
            'Content-Type': 'application/json'
        })

        aiohttpTCPConn = aiohttp.TCPConnector(
            family=socket.AF_INET,
            verify_ssl=False,
            loop=self.loop,
            limit=2,
            limit_per_host=2
        )
        sem = asyncio.Semaphore(value=5, loop=self.loop)

        async with sem:
            async with aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=aiohttpTCPConn, loop=self.loop) as s:
                #timeout is request time, not query execution time
                response = await s.post(QUERY_URL, data=payload, headers=headers, params=None, ssl=False, timeout=60)
                content = await response.json()

                if 299 >= response.status >= 200:
                    if content['jobComplete']:
                        return {
                            'status': 'success',
                            'content': content
                        }
                    else:
                        return await self.getQueryJobResults(content['jobReference']['jobId'], s)

        return {
            'status': 'error',
            'content': content
        }


    async def getQueryJobResults(self, jobID, session):

issue_title: Why is creating a ClientSession outside of an event loop "dangerous"?
issue_body:
If you create a `ClientSession` outside of a running event loop, you get:

    Creating a client session outside of coroutine is a very dangerous idea

Which comes from [here](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/3dfc12379afc1657516e297fdd483c3b8a0db031/aiohttp/client.py#L122-L123)

**First and foremost, I would really like to know *why* this is dangerous.**

Ideally, I'd like to have the actual warning inform me, so that I would not need to dig through the code to determine it. But looking at the code, I'm still not sure: why is this dangerous?

To explain my use case, hopefully: at program initialization we create a few global `ClientSession`s, and pass those into various instances of things that require them. I don't really see anything inherent in `ClientSession` that should prevent it from initializing itself synchronously, and this pattern (creating needed connection pools at a high level context in the program, and passing them, typically via constructor, to lower level contexts) is essentially how I think anyone would expect a connection-pool like object to operate. (Now, I'm writing server-side code, so these pools have a lifetime of effectively forever; we shut them down when the server shuts down. I can of course see a more client-side code wanting a very brief `with ClientSession()`, but that isn't my use-case.)

Idempotency is a concept new to many charm authors. Many regular sysadmin actions are not idempotent by default. Charm authors need to write a lot of boilerplate to create idempotent handlers.

We need to have more documentation explaining this, giving examples for how to write idempotent code, and we need an "approved" way to deal with the idempotent issue.

This issue has been solved by config mgmt tools years ago, so the preferred way is to leverage an existing tool for that. I don't think charms.reactive needs to reinvent the wheel in this area, as [the vision](https://github.com/juju-solutions/charms.reactive/blob/f8a250e8939c5591866b4da5b0b7d717396a28e2/2.0-proposal/VISION.md) states

> charms.reactive is a tool for **coordinating** config management. **It is not a config management tool!**

Charms.reactive is used to figure out what state the server needs to be, config mgmt tools are used to actually get the server into that state.

I've uses Puppet before in charms, such as in [the OpenVPN charm](https://github.com/tengu-team/layer-openvpn), and I really like it. I also found that Puppet is a lot quicker than Python or Bash, especially no-ops. Puppet is also used in all Bigtop charms from the Big Data ecosystem, and many other charmers use it so we have a community to support and test an "official" charms.reactive -> Puppet integration. The [layer-puppet-base](https://github.com/tengu-team/layer-puppet-base) might be a good starting point for this.

from f5.bigip import ManagementRoot
mgmt = ManagementRoot("x.x.x.x", "admin", "admin")

Hi,
You've changed ```get_order_totals``` call from (1.3)
```
total = self.get_order_totals( basket, shipping_charge=shipping_charge)
```
to (master branch)
```
total = self.get_order_totals( basket, shipping_charge=shipping_charge, **kwargs)
```
In apps.checkout.session.CheckoutSessionMixin.
So, the problem is when you call build_submission with basket passed in kwargs.
You are given ```django oscar get order_totals() got multiple values for argument 'basket'``` error when call ```get_order_totals``` because basek is passed twice ones as positional arg the second in kwargs.

For my own workaround I've just removed ``**kwargs```. The right way how to solve it is up to you.

Of course it is possible that I'm wrong and I try to use build_submission in strange way.

Regards
Jarek

issue_title: Fix missing help_label in noninteractive menu

issue_body:
Currently, when you run commands like `letsencrypt --help install`, you get the following output:
```
$ letsencrypt --help install  
usage: 
  letsencrypt [SUBCOMMAND] [options] [domains]

The Let's Encrypt agent can obtain and install HTTPS/TLS/SSL certificates.  By
default, it will attempt to use a webserver both for obtaining and installing
the cert.  

positional arguments:
  SUBCOMMAND
    run                 Obtain a certificate and install.
    auth                Authenticate & obtain cert, but do not install it.
    install             Install a previously obtained cert in a server.
    revoke              Revoke a previously obtained certificate.
    rollback            Rollback server configuration changes made during
                        install.
    config_changes      Show changes made to server config during installation
    plugins             List server software plugins.

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  -c CONFIG_FILE, --config CONFIG_FILE
                        config file path (default: None)

install:
  Options for modifying how a cert is deployed
```
This to me doesn't seem very helpful. The problem is arguments shared between subcommands. Most of these common flags are now in the `paths` argument subgroup. I think ideally, the common arguments should not be duplicated when running `letsencrypt --help all`, but should be shown when running help for a specific subcommand. I do not believe this behavior is achievable with `argparse` and adds a fair amount of complexity, so we shouldn't worry about doing anything like this for now.

Instead, a simple thing we could do is update the description for the relevant subgroups to point users towards the `paths` group. I'm not sure what other simple fixes could be done here, but I don't think the help for commands like `install` and `revoke` does much to help the user right now.

issue_title: Bad request while using standalone plugin
issue_body:
Hi there,
I'm running letsencrypt on Ubuntu 14.04.3 behind an installation of haproxy.
I've configured haproxy to forward every request on port 443 containing `acme-challenge` to the letsencrypt standalone server running on port 444 which I changed with `--dvsni-port 444`.

I built a script that automatically refreshes all of my certificates **which worked flawlessly** for my configuration until now.
Obviously the parameter `--dvsni-port` is no longer available, so I changed the call to use `--tls-sni-01-port 444`.

However, this does not seem to work:
`/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt certonly --email admin@***** --domains api.*****.com --standalone --standalone-supported-challenges tls-sni-01 --tls-sni-01-port 444 --debug`

It returns the following:
```
FailedChallenges: Failed authorization procedure. api.*****.com (tls-sni-01): urn:acme:error:unauthorized :: The client lacks sufficient authorization :: Correct zName not found for TLS SNI challenge. Found api.*****.com

The following 'urn:acme:error:unauthorized' errors were reported by the server:

Domains: api.*****.com
Error: The client lacks sufficient authorization
```
In my haproxy log I just get this:
```
Dec 27 17:54:09 localhost haproxy[21317]: *****:35127 [27/Dec/2015:17:54:09.403] https_relay~ https_relay/<NOSRV> -1/-1/-1/-1/298 400 187 - - CR-- 0/0/0/0/0 0/0 "<BADREQ>"
```
The standalone server runs on port 444 (since the log says `DEBUG:letsencrypt.plugins.standalone:Stopping server at 0.0.0.0:444...`) and the request comes in on 443 (otherwise it would not be handled by my haproxy frontend `https_relay`), so everything is as I would expect.

**Does anyone have any idea what could cause this bad request?**

issue_body:
According to Wikipedia [YYYYMM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Calendar_dates) is explicitly *not* supported in ISO-8601, so we should drop support for it. Hopefully this does not add undue complication into the algorithm.

See #489 for initial isoparse implementation.

issue_title: fields2parameters - default_in body - array with single parameter
issue_body:
Why does the fields2parameters method return an array with a single parameter?

``` python
    def fields2parameters(fields, schema=None, spec=None, use_refs=True, dump=True,
                          default_in='body', name='body', required=False):
        """Return an array of OpenAPI parameters given a mapping between field names and
        :class:`Field <marshmallow.Field>` objects. If `default_in` is "body", then return an array
        of a single parameter; else return an array of a parameter for each included field in
        the :class:`Schema <marshmallow.Schema>`.

        https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#parameterObject
        """
        if default_in == 'body':
            if schema is not None:
                # Prevent circular import
                from apispec.ext.marshmallow import resolve_schema_dict
                prop = resolve_schema_dict(spec, schema, dump=dump)
            else:
                prop = fields2jsonschema(fields, spec=spec, use_refs=use_refs, dump=dump)

            return [{
                'in': default_in,
                'required': required,
                'name': name,
                'schema': prop,
            }]
```
What do I do when I have a Schema with all fields in the body except 1, which is in the header.
How do I accomplish the right parameters? Meaning, one body parameter and one header parameter.

Thanks

Hi,


I want a field that can either `fields.Str()` or `fields.List(fields.Str())`. I don't want to have to use https://github.com/Bachmann1234/marshmallow-polyfield because it seems to be more Schema / nested option oriented. Can anyone point me in the direction of being able to do the above?

Thanks!

issue_title: Do make_instance() and custom post_load-methods clash?
issue_body:
Hey,

I'm working on a Flask app using sqlalchemy, marshmallow and marshmallow-sqlalchemy. I implemented a basic API, using pre_load and post-load decorators for some models to change/adjust fields. 

Sometimes I need to create additional sqlA-objects based on the data and for this I use the post_load-decorator. 

(from the docs, as illustration)
```
class AuthorSchema(ModelSchema):
    class Meta:
        model = Author

    @post_load
    def create_stuff(self, data):
        print(type(data))
```

I would expect the `data` variable to be an instance of the SQLA-model, which works for 2 of my models. 

Now, for a third, I get a dictionary. The dictionary already contains the SQLA-instances for nested fields, but is still a dictionary. Digging into the marshmallow-sqlalchemy code and adding some `print`-statements in `ModelSchema.make_instance` showed that this method works correctly (returning the model instance), but I realized that my print-statements in my own `post_load`-method are printed **before** the ones I added to the `make_instance`-method. 
Doing the same for one of the two working schemata the print in my method is printed after the `make_instance`-one.

So, my question: Is it possible that there is some kind of race condition happening between both decorated methods? I found a warning regarding the execution order in the marshmallow-docs (https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/extending.html#pre-post-processor-invocation-order), and both methods are decorated with the `post_load`-decorator - once in my code and once in the ModelSchema-class.

If this seems unreasonable, has anyone any idea why I don't get the expected SQLA-model instance?

And of course, last but not least, thank you for this library!

issue_title: Adding support for printing a query in DIMACS format
issue_body:
Recently a user (@awanish-pandey) of [klee](https://github.com/klee/klee) enquired about outputting queries (in the QF_ABV logic) in the [DIMACS format](http://www.domagoj-babic.com/uploads/ResearchProjects/Spear/dimacs-cnf.pdf).

Given that KLEE uses Z3 as one of its solvers I was wondering if it would possible to do this via Z3.

I had a quick grep through Z3's codebase and noticed that internally this is already supported in two places.

* [In the SAT solver](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/5066bd01f799e8a7f2af006f205de0c0847bdeee/src/sat/sat_solver.cpp#L2912)

* [On Goals](
https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/5066bd01f799e8a7f2af006f205de0c0847bdeee/src/tactic/goal.cpp#L431)

I was wondering if it would be possible to expose this functionality from the C API, and if yes how this should be done.

A problem that immediately comes to mind is that assertions might not even be reducible to a SAT problem so if a new function was added to the C API it would need to deal with this case in some way.

One place this could go is as a function `Z3_goal_to_dimacs_string()` that operates on a goal and produces the string iff the goal is already in CNF form, otherwise raise the API error handler. This would require that the client apply the necessary tactics to get the problem into CNF first, this would also require a `is-cnf` probe to be implemented so that the client knows when they can use `Z3_goal_to_dimacs_string()` without raising an error.

This would probably quite cumbersome and error prone though so a function `Z3_solver_to_dimacs()` string could be implemented instead that does applies the necessary tactics internally and just returns an empty string if the problem cannot be reduced to SAT.

Any thoughts?

issue_body:
Here's my query encoded in the muz format:
```
(set-logic HORN)
(declare-rel $END_QUERY ())
(declare-rel INV_REL
 ((_ BitVec 32) Int (_ BitVec 32) (_ BitVec 32)
  (_ BitVec 32) Int (_ BitVec 32) (_ BitVec 32)))
(declare-var i Int)
(declare-var i.2 Int)
(declare-var n (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-var n.0 (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-var x (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-var x.3 (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-var y (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-var y.4 (_ BitVec 32))

(rule
 (=>
  (and (= n.0 n)
   (INV_REL n.0 i.2 x.3 (_ bv0 32) n i x
    (_ bv0 32))
   (and (>= i.2 7) (>= i 7)) (not (= x.3 x)))
  $END_QUERY))
  
(rule
 (=>
  (and (and (< i.2 7) (>= i 7))
   (INV_REL n.0 i.2 x.3 y.4 n i x y))
  $END_QUERY))
  
(rule
 (=>
  (and (and (>= i.2 7) (< i 7))
   (INV_REL n.0 i.2 x.3 y.4 n i x y))
  $END_QUERY))
  
(rule
 (=> (= n.0 n)
  (INV_REL n.0 0 (_ bv0 32) (_ bv0 32) n 0 (_ bv0 32) (_ bv0 32))))
  
(rule
 (let ((x.3$2 (bvor (bvshl x.3 (_ bv4 32)) (bvudiv y.4 n.0))))
  (let ((i.2$2 (+ i.2 1)))
   (let ((x$2 (bvor (bvshl x (_ bv4 32)) (bvudiv y n))))
    (let ((i$2 (+ i 1)))
     (=>
      (and (and (< i.2 7) (< i 7))
       (INV_REL n.0 i.2 x.3 y.4 n i x y))
      (INV_REL n.0 i.2$2 x.3$2 y.4 n i$2 x$2 y)))))))
      
(query $END_QUERY)
```
The expected answer is `unsat`. I tried `pdr`, `gpdr`, `spacer` and `duality`, and all of them would hang on this simple example.

This problem originally comes from the application domain of equivalence checking: I want to verify the equivalence of the following two procedures:
```c
uint32_t f0(uint32_t n) {
  uint32_t x = 0, y = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < 7; i++) {
    x = ((x << 4) | (y / n));
  }
  return x;
}

uint32_t f1(uint32_t n_0) {
  uint32_t x_3 = 0, y_4 = 0;
  for (int i_2 = 0; i_2 < 7; i_2++) {
    x_3 = ((x_3 << 4) | (y_4 / n));
  }
  return x_3;
}
```
To me this seems to be an easy problem: the unknown predicate `INV_REL` could just be set to `x == x_3` to solve the query. I'm not a horn solver expert so I'm not sure what makes the problem so hard for the horn engine. Is there any option that can be used to get better performance? Thanks in advance.

issue_title: Maybe consider a "visibility" info namespace for "hidden" files
issue_body:
Hi Will,

now that the typing is merged, I was thinking about adding support for a `visibility` (or `hidden`) info namespace, with a simple `hidden` key.

If that does seem like a good idead, I'll start working on a PR.

### Concerned filesystems

In the core library, we could probably support:

* `ZipFS` (I read somewhere it supports a *hidden* flag in the external attributes of each entry)
* `OSFS` (I'll detail my idea below).

I could also add support for hidden files once the namespace is stabilised to:

* `ISOFS` in `fs.archive`
* `SMBFS` in `fs.smbfs`

### Semantics of OSFS

So here's what the situation about hidden files:

* Windows has a special attribute for hidden files
* UNIX has no attribute, but files starting with a dot are considered hidden
* MacOS follow the UNIX convention, but also has the `stat.UF_HIDDEN` flag to hide files as well

But if we were to support it directly that way, there would be an ambiguity on MacOS where we would not really know if a file is just a dotted file or a dotted file with the `UF_HIDDEN` flag as well.

So instead, here's what I propose:

* on Windows, files are hidden only with the hidden flag
* on MacOS, files are hidden only with the `stat.UF_HIDDEN` flag
* on Linux, files are never hidden

and then addind a `HideDottedMeta` wrapper that:

* set `hidden` to `True` if the file is a dotted file
* raise a `ValueError` when trying to `setinfo` with the `hidden` flag set to avoid ambiguity

Our workflow w.r.t. versioning has worked like this up to this point:

1. Work work work.
2. When students need to receive the updates, bump up the minor version
(eg. `0.3.2 -> 0.3.3`) and release on Pypi.
3. Repeat.

Ideally, our workflow would look more like:

1. Add features to master, being careful not to introduce backwards-incompatible
changes until major releases (eg. `0.6.1 -> 1.0.0`).
2. After each feature is merged, add a description in a changelog for each release.
3. When we deem it's time for a release:
  1. Push the release to Pypi
  2. Push a new version of the docs (so that someone working with a specific version
  has the correct docs for that version).
  3. Push the release to a Github branch.

issue_title: Allow to use asynchronous manager RPC like futures
issue_body:
The asynchronous mode is not very useful at the moment as all the processing as you then need to wait, check for errors and do vendor specific transformations by hand.

IMO the synchronous part of the _request method of the RPC class should be moved to a `result()` method so the RPC can behave like a future.
Then you could do:
```
manager.async_mode = True
manager.get_config(source="candidate").result()
```
and it would behave the same as the synchronous version.

When building ncclient with python-3.x in a C locale (i.e. not UTF-8), it fails with:
````
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "setup.py", line 32, in <module>
    long_description = file.read()
  File "/usr/pkg/lib/python3.5/encodings/ascii.py", line 26, in decode
    return codecs.ascii_decode(input, self.errors)[0]
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xd0 in position 4335: ordinal not in range(128)
````
The problem is that one of the contributors listed in `README.rst` is using UTF-8 characters.

issue_title: kinto.plugins.default_bucket doesn't work with the Postgresql backend.

issue_body:
With the [accounts plugin](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/tree/master/kinto/plugins/accounts) it is now possible to handle user directly in Kinto.

However currently the steps are to activate the user creation for everyone, create an administrator account and then enable the creation of account only for that superuser. 

Each configuration change being a server restart.

For deployment it is something tricky to do. It would be convenient to have a `kinto createsuperuser` command that works a bit like the [`django-admin` command](https://docs.djangoproject.com/fr/1.11/ref/django-admin/#createsuperuser) and allow the creation of the super user from the server.

issue_title: Indexing the result of multilevel & multidimensional transforms
issue_body:
There have been a few proposals for alternative representations for the output of a transform in #93, and I think this needs some more discussion. It gets rapidly complex, when for example wavelet packet transforms are used.

The following are some examples of weird cases that might need to be accessed:
- Transform that is approximate in the first dimension, detail in the second
- Transform that is at the first level approximate in the first dimension, detail in the second, then at the second level detail at the first dimension and detail in the second

I can't see any easy solutions to this, but any ideas to discuss would be great.

issue_title: Some item stats are counted twice
issue_body:
For example, ItemStats for Doran's Blade returns 0.06, when it should be 0.03. From my understanding, I assume this is because 3% is scraped from the item description, and 3% is also returned from the API (`'PercentLifeStealMod': 0.03`) which are then added together

```python
>>> x = next(x for x in riotapi.get_items() if x.name == "Doran's Blade")
>>> x.stats.life_steal
0.06
```

I'm assuming this is only a problem on items that offer stuff as a non-passive stat.

issue_title: _get_column_info() missing 1 required positional argument: 'comment' in SQLAlchemy 1.2.0
issue_body:
https://github.com/sqlalchemy-redshift/sqlalchemy-redshift/blob/92852b7d2c720e7a088a788203b71c0c8447fa2e/sqlalchemy_redshift/dialect.py#L607

This breaks, because this was added:

http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/changelog/changelog_12.html#change-3f38508932257c303017b1276f285c39

When it's called from `get_columns()`:

https://github.com/sqlalchemy-redshift/sqlalchemy-redshift/blob/92852b7d2c720e7a088a788203b71c0c8447fa2e/sqlalchemy_redshift/dialect.py#L406-L409

Because `comment` is missing from there.

Hi all

I'd like to make the capitalization of feature names in matminer consistent. We can do:

* everything lower case (e.g. "band center") except for acroynms (e.g. "formation energy FERE")

issue_title: A new way to write ObjectType with python3's annotation
Fragments: <Type> can never be of type <Type>

issue_title: Prefetch_related in root resolver not preserved in children resolvers
Incorrect response format for batched queries
issue_body:
I've been working with `graphene-django` for the last 3 weeks, and have been implementing it with `apollo-client` on the frontend. I just switched on batched queries, and determined that `graphene-django` is trying to return a list of responses in the following format:

`
response = [
{
  id,
  payload,
  status
},
...
]
`

I can't seem to find any documentation that indicates that this should be the format for batched responses. As it is, this breaks `apollo-client`. I propose just returning a list of responses to adhere to the expected format.

issue_title: Add -e option to CPT to pass environment variables
issue_body:
With most CI services, setting the environment variables for CPT in a declarative way is elegant and great: 

Jenkins example: 
```
                    environment {
                        SOME_VAR = 'some_value'
                    }
```

However, when trying to run CPT builds manually during testing, or for organizations with homegrown CI (scripts) , users have to rely on setting the environment variable commands manually.   This is troublesome because windows uses `set VAR=VAL` while linux uses `export VAR=VAL`.  Also, it can be less deterministic and less easy to debug when looking at a log as to what values were actually used. 

Conan, like docker, supports the `-e` value for similar reasons.  It's a nice, cross-platform feature, and should be easily to bring over into CPT.  

Also, this came out of a conversation from slack where a user wanted to be able to pass parameters to `python build.py`, and didn't much like the idea of the environment variables (related to some degree to the reasons given above).  I demonstrated the value of the environment variables, but did like the idea of adding `-e` option, which effectively allows the user to pass any supported conan variable as a pseudo command-line argument, without having to deal with argparse. Nothing against argparse, it's just a lot of work and thought to reconcile a third source for variable values with the two existing ones (constructor and environment variables).

issue_title: Add indicator what DB is being used
issue_body:
Thanks to @JochenABC for the suggestion

> In the standard output: Indicate wether the API Key or the (older)
free db was used for this scan. For us it is difficult to determine
since we run safety check --key=${SAFETY_CHECK_API_KEY} in a CI job and
the env var gets injected by CI.

> Env var injection not set in CI because of misconfig -> "safety check
--key=" is run -> we run on public db

> Env var injection works -> safety check --key=verysrect is run -> we run
on the latest db

Currently OpenThread's MAC implementation retransmits MAC Frames using the same sequence (DSN) but different Frame Counter (in Auxilary header). However my understanding is that Frame Counter has to be the same for subsequent retransmissions.

The 802.15.4 specification is not so clear about it, but i believe it is possible to make a deduction that current behavior is not correct (Transmission and Retransmission chapters). Also all implementations that I know retransmit frames using the same Frame Counter which looks logical for me.

The one obvious problem was when i have tested OpenThread nodes in dense network and i often received 2 ICMP Echo Reply (on one Echo Request) instead of one, whenever ACK for first Echo Reply hasn't been detected on other side. Normaly Frame Counter guarantees that this situation will not occur, and second Echo Reply will be simply discarded on MAC Layer (due to reply attack protection).

I'm new for raspberry pi and I am setting up the development environment on raspbian following the tutorial.
when I executed the "./script/bootstrap" it told me

and then I checked the bootstrap file and apt list, there is no clang-format-5.0 in apt list.

So I replaced the 5.0 with 4.0.

As a result, when I make file, there are a lot of nothing to be done error. and the test failed.

Can some one help?

issue_title: missing check for duplicates when appending to _subscriptions list in application client
issue_body:
Our application creates a client using iot-python/src/ibmiotf/application.py.  It then periodicallly, creates a connection and subscribes to a topic using subscribeToDeviceEvents().  (In each of these subscribe requests, the application specifices the same topic.) After receiving messages, the client is disconnected.

We are finding that on each successive call to subscribeToDeviceEvents(), additional subscribe requests are received by the MQTT server.  It looks like this is because each time subscribe() is called in subscribeToDeviceEvents(), the topic/qos for the request are added to the _subscriptions list.  It seems that before appending a given topic/qos to this list, the application client should check first to see whether the subscription is already in the list.

issue_title: OperationFuture should not use fork and use multiprocessing
issue_body:
Our present implementation of `OperationFuture.add_done_callback` has two branches with vastly different behavior:

1. If the operation is already done, it will immediately invoke the callback on the main process. This is in line with `asyncio` and `concurrent.futures`.
2. If the operation is *not* already done, it will *fork* and spawn a separate process to do a blocking poll on the operation. When the operation is complete, it will invoke the callback **on the forked process**.

The second behavior is problematic for several reasons:

1. It violates principle of least astonishment. A user does not expect their process to be forked and that the callback could potentially be called on a forked process. In fact, `concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor` goes [out of its way](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/0edffa3073b551ffeca34952529e7b292f1bd350/Lib/concurrent/futures/process.py#L313) to ensure that callbacks are executed on the process that starts the pool, not in the processes owned by the pool.
2. Forking behavior is undesirable. First and foremost, it causes and entire copy of the process just to handle a simple non-blocking poll. Additionally, forking a multithreaded process is [problematic](https://docs.python.org/3/library/multiprocessing.html#contexts-and-start-methods) and gRPC's c-core uses threads by design. Finally, gRPC does not support being forked and [barfs](https://gist.github.com/jonparrott/9c1d229adb43388fb81afca29af28c72) when the connection gets corrupted (by some saving grace, either our retries or gRPC's own logic, gRPC can re-establish the connection in the forked process).

We should just use a thread for this. There's no benefit to us using multiprocessing for this case. 

Bonus: we can drop `dill` as a dependency.

By some measure, we should be able to provide good performance on classic datasets to show that the pipes are in place to scale to larger examples.

The classic overused dataset everyone uses is [MNIST](http://yann.lecun.com/exdb/mnist/). I'd rather see [fashion-MNIST](https://github.com/zalandoresearch/fashion-mnist) or [EMNIST](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1702.05373v1.pdf).

Good data loading utilities for all of these datasets are with PyTorch via [torchvision.datasets](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/torchvision/datasets.html).

I have found that people using our system have no concept of what kubernetes is, and frankly there isn't really a requirement for them to know the details other than it is a kind of scheduler.

But then I get questions like "How do I get the worker logs" and my answer includes a call to `cluster.pods()`. At which point I have to explain what a pod is and its relationship to a worker.

I would find it useful to remove the kubernetes specific language for end users. It would make more sense for them to call `cluster.workers()`.

I would be keen to hear others thoughts on the matter.

issue_title: Discontinuing support for Python 2.7 on April 15, 2018
issue_body:
Supporting multiple old versions of Python is becoming more and more burdensome as good features get added to newer versions of Python which don't get back-ported to older versions.

I don't want to abandon support for versions of Python that users are actively using.  But I also don't want to waste developer time supporting versions nobody cares about.

So whoever reads this, please feel free to add a comment and say what versions of Python you care about ``cmd2`` supporting now and in the near future.

issue_title: combine domain.tld and wildcard *.domain.tld in one certificate
issue_body:
I ran into an issue while testing the new wildcard feature of the acme-v02 endpoint in manual mode.
I was trying to create a certificate including a domain `example.com` and a wildcard `*.example.com`
Now the certbot runs the auth hook for each entered canonical name and THEN runs the validation.
So it's creating the TXT-Record for `_acme-challenge.example.com` for the first domain and then creates the same record with the second keys before validating the domains and then the validation fails for at least one CN since letsencrypt isn't able to differenciate which key is for which CN.
Is it possible do make certbot run the validations seperately, like: run auth-hook, validate, cleanup, run auth-hook, validate, cleanup?
Creating to different certificates with one CN `example.com` and the other one with `*.example.com` runs without any problems, but i would like to have both in one certificate if that is even possible.

## My operating system is (include version):
Tested on Ubuntu 14.04 and Ubuntu 16.04

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
Tested with certbot-auto and the one from the APT packet manager

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
Command:
```
certbot-auto certonly \
    --manual \
    --preferred-challenge dns-01 \
    --manual-auth-hook /my/manual/auth-hook \
    --manual-cleanup-hook /my/manual/cleanup-hook \
    --server https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory' \
    -d domain.com \
    -d *.domain.com
```
## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
As described above, it ran all the auth-hooks at first, then all validations, then all cleanup-hooks.
I expected it to run the first auth-hook, then the first validation, then the first cleanup, and then continues with the next canonical name.

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available):
###### Logs are stored in `/var/log/letsencrypt` by default. Feel free to redact domains, e-mail and IP addresses as you see fit.

## Here is the relevant nginx server block or Apache virtualhost for the domain I am configuring:
I deploy my certs manually, so the nginx server blocks are not relevant for this issue.

issue_title: Apache SSL cipher settings are old, no ChaCha20
issue_labels: [{'id': 258836903, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20apache', 'name': 'area: apache', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 214927649, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20security', 'name': 'area: security', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 281347824, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/has%20pr', 'name': 'has pr', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The Nginx plugin's `options-ssl-nginx.conf` file uses Mozilla's current intermediate SSL cipher configuration.

The Apache plugin probably did too... except it hasn't been updated since 2014: 2faacc1b43786edd5386305f9cffec376b5a5d26

Should Certbot's Apache settings be updated?

The main difference is that the new configuration adds ChaCha20 cipher suites. (It also removes a few things.)

Should this wait until after further documentation/feature improvements in #4830?

If so, how about a stopgap patch to add ChaCha20 without removing anything?

Differences with OpenSSL 1.0.2 or 1.1.0:

* Adds ChaCha20. (Yay!)
* Adds newer 3DES cipher suites with key exchange/signature algorithms other than RSA/RSA. (Probably doesn't matter. Anything that supports ECDHE-ECDSA ought to support a better cipher than 3DES. ) (Edit: Mozilla suggests that EDH-RSA 3DES is useful, though,.)
* Removes AES-CCM. (OpenSSL 1.1. Probably only included inadvertently.)
* Removes Camellia. (Perfectly nice cipher, but everybody uses AES.)
* Removes some static DH and SRP key exchange cipher suites. (Probably only included by accident, mostly or entirely ignored by servers and clients.)
* Changes the order of some things.

issue_title: _handle_missing_api_key () and _handle_check_response () pass the wrong value to start_response()
issue_body:
I sent a request with no API key to an API that requires an API key, and I caught the following error.
( I expected HTTP status code 401, but actually 500 was returned. )

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/base/data/home/runtimes/python27_experiment/python27_lib/versions/1/google/appengine/runtime/wsgi.py", line 267, in Handle
    result = handler(dict(self._environ), self._StartResponse)
  File "/base/data/home/apps/b~gae-service-samples/cloud-endpoints-framework:1.401219152325622663/lib/endpoints_management/control/wsgi.py", line 199, in __call__
    return self._application(environ, start_response)
  File "/base/data/home/apps/b~gae-service-samples/cloud-endpoints-framework:1.401219152325622663/lib/endpoints_management/control/wsgi.py", line 606, in __call__
    response = self._application(environ, start_response)
  File "/base/data/home/apps/b~gae-service-samples/cloud-endpoints-framework:1.401219152325622663/lib/endpoints_management/control/wsgi.py", line 282, in __call__
    error_msg = self._handle_missing_api_key(app_info, start_response)
  File "/base/data/home/apps/b~gae-service-samples/cloud-endpoints-framework:1.401219152325622663/lib/endpoints_management/control/wsgi.py", line 411, in _handle_missing_api_key
    start_response(error_msg, [])
  File "/base/data/home/runtimes/python27_experiment/python27_lib/versions/1/google/appengine/runtime/wsgi.py", line 180, in _StartResponse
    (_GetTypeName(status), status))
InvalidResponseError: status must be a str, got 'unicode' (u'401 Method does not allow callers without established identity. Please use an API key or other form of API consumer identity to call this API.')
```

I think that it is the cause of this error that _handle_missing_api_key() and _handle_check_response() pass the wrong value to start_response().
In the specification of wsgi, start_response() expects str to be passed to the first parameter, but now unicode is passed.

## Problem Definition

This is not a really serious problem, but we should also think of introducing an upper limit for the contract's `settle_timeout` property.

The only reason why we would need to do that is to protect users from themselves, by not letting them enter a `settle_timeout` close to `2**256 - 1` and then have either [this](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ee25ac5f3f9161d196074a65ace75310e5cbf0e0/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L52) or [this](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ee25ac5f3f9161d196074a65ace75310e5cbf0e0/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L56) modifier check overflow and have their channel botched with the tokens trapped in there forever.

## Solution

Provide an upper limit for `settle_timeout` and enforce it in the contract.
